Observation Details: Heard one individual singing just SE of parking  lot (heard just before I got out of my car), right away. Then saw a different adult male fly low into dense shrubs along NW edge of parking lot. Then saw and heard another adult male foraging under dense shrubs, then coming out onto first unpaved trail. Simultaneously heard another individual singing to the NE. Then saw one adult female individual on ground just behind the first Fox Sparrow. After going to NW corner and back, also heard and saw an adult male with (probably the same) female individual. Later, heard one individual singing to the east, another to the SW, while hearing a different pair [saw the adult male on round in dense saplings] making high-treble chip calls nearby (observed from west end of boardwalk in far NE section). ... Finally, saw a female individual along west edge of parking lot, where I saw the first male. ... (Conservative total of at least 12 individuals for the outing.) ... Background Species: Corvus ossifragus -- Fish Crow; Passer domesticus -- House Sparrow; Baeolophus bicolor -- Tufted Titmouse
Media notes: Both singing in close proximity in different holly trees in my back yard. I couldn't see them but saw the female fly from one holly tree to the other and the singing stopped. This is the first day I have seen or heard cardinals in my yard in weeks. It's the coldest day of the winter so far (14F) and I was surprised two hear two birds singing today.
Observation Details: I believe this is a duet between a male and female cardinal--very exciting! Initially I thought it must be two males vying for territory, but they sounded so close (both in my not-very-big yard) and I thought that was odd because usually the cardinals pick different yards to sing from. I had not seen or heard cardinals in my yard all winter and for two males to show up on the same day, the coldest day of the winter so far at 14 degrees F, really seemed strange.  Then I saw a female fly from a holly where one bird was singing toward hollies on the other side of the yard where the other bird was singing from (I didn’t see that bird) and the singing stopped. That’s why I think it was a pair dueting. Contributed for the female bird song project: femalebirdsong.org

I recorded this because it's a song that, from a distance, I frequently mistake for a Carolina Wren's. Williston Road traffic and calling frogs in the background.
Observation Details: Three singing. I recorded one along the service road because, from a distance, I initially mistook its song for that of a Carolina Wren. Some wrens have a particularly fast-moving song that my poor hearing often misinterprets as that of a cardinal, since it hits only one of its three notes hard and I can't hear the other two. I assumed that the same thing was happening here, but when I looked it actually was a cardinal.

Drama at the cardinal nest today.  There is an active cardinal nest in a crepe myrtle behind our bedroom window. I have been watching the parents tend this for some three weeks now and have been wondering when we might see some new arrivals. Today was the day. At one moment I saw a new squeaker fledge and land frightenedly on the bricks at the side of the house, maman close and helping the little one get to the red-tips across a short space of lawn. Eventually succeeded.  Just after this fun I saw two adult cardinals (a couple) along with their juvenile (I presume) fly into the front of the crepe myrtle. This is an unusual approach and their arrival precipitated considerable agitation among the leaves. I can only imagine that this was a different couple here to attack? and/or disrupt? the nesting couple. Soon I note the three cardinals flew off, drama over.   Soon the parents were again flying back and forth between the nest and the red-tips where little squeaker was.  I must conclude that one little one has fledged and at least one other remains in the nest. Looking through the bedroom window (stealthily) I could not confirm this.

Observation Details: Both singing. I recorded one odd song that sounds for all the world as if the cardinal were engaging in a duet with a Great Crested Flycatcher. The note at 0:01 sounds like a GCFL, and is followed by twelve seconds (0:03-0:14) of cardinal song - though a GCFL note seems to end that sequence. Between 0:18 and 0:28 there are three sequences of notes that sound like GCFL to me. But then, from 0:46 to 0:50 it gives a series of "GCFL notes" that accelerate smoothly into a series of cardinal notes. So although there does seem to be a GCFL in the background, the song itself is all cardinal.

Observation Details: Heard one individual singing just SE of parking  lot (heard just before I got out of my car), right away. Then saw a different adult male fly low into dense shrubs along NW edge of parking lot. Then saw and heard another adult male foraging under dense shrubs, then coming out onto first unpaved trail. Simultaneously heard another individual singing to the NE. Then saw one adult female individual on ground just behind the first Fox Sparrow. After going to NW corner and back, also heard and saw an adult male with (probably the same) female individual. Later, heard one individual singing to the east, another to the SW, while hearing a different pair [saw the adult male on round in dense saplings] making high-treble chip calls nearby (observed from west end of boardwalk in far NE section). ... Finally, saw a female individual along west edge of parking lot, where I saw the first male. ... (Conservative total of at least 12 individuals for the outing.) ...  (Posted audio recordings of one adult male just north of boardwalk at west section of marshy area.) ... (Posted another audio recording of a different male singing, along east side of far north section.)

Heard one individual singing loudly from along first side-trail, hearing it from a tree at the edge of the open area. Saw a different adult male individual flying past, while listening to the first. Then heard another and saw another adult male individual singing from arched branches in woods in NE section, while hearing another individual singing just a bit farther SE. Flushed a female individual making high-treble chip calls. Then heard or saw a few more in SE and south sections. ... (Posted audio recording of adult male individual singing, [overlapping with female RWBL "chuck" calls], along east edge of pond.)

Media notes: Male and female Cardinals feeding their young in rounds. Cardinal hatchlings vocalizing when parent arrives with food.
Observation Details: Sighted male and female alternately feeding young. Lady arrived with mouth full of insects, fed young, flew off; then male arrived with mouth full of insects, fed young, flew off. Also noticed parents didn't take a direct route to nest. Landed on edge of my front porch, flew into Rose of Sharon, then hopped on 2-3 branches inside the bush before approaching the nest. A safety route to throw off potential predators? Hatchlings vocalized when parents arrived at nest and would go quiet when they flew off again. Audio attached.

Observation Details: Bird was singing from Bohde's yard several times.  I heard it while I was sitting at the cabin.   By the time I got my device to make a recording the bird only sang two more times. Recorded the bird singing at about :54 and 1:00  into the recording.  The bird did not sing again nor make itself visible for photos.    Before making this recording,  I felt sure I heard the longer song of a Carolina Wren.  This short song  I was able to record leans to Cardinal.     Given that I could not get a recording of the long sone, given that I could not locate a CAWR, and given this recording leans to Cardinal, I am ticking this observation as NOCA.    Thanks to both Adam and Caleb for their review.

Media notes: Singing from inside a small white cedar that happened to be right next to a compost barrel. I put my phone inside the barrel, which made for some great amplification. Best level of detail I've had for a cardinal song -- from 1 kHz all the way up to 19 kHz.  This cardinal was trading off singing with a second, but one so far away the phone couldn't pick it up. From what I understood from Catchpole and Slater's book on birdsong, some of the hesitation in the singing would be due to this distance as well. They need to hear each other's information, not just produce their own.

Media notes: On entend un écho après le chant du mâle. Est-ce de l'écho ou un autre individu qui chante plus loin ?
Observation Details: Avant de quitter le secteur, nous avons recensé les oiseaux aux mangeoires au 1302 rang Ste-Anne. Par la suite, nous nous sommes dirigés vers la voiture. Avant que la dernière portière de la voiture ne se ferme, nous entendons soudain un Cardinal rouge chanter! Nous partons à sa rencontre où nous avons pu l'observer quelques minutes. Le mâle chantait très souvent, changeant de perchoir et parfois, changeant de territoire. La dernière fois que nous l'avons vu, il chantait dans un bosquet de conifères derrière la résidence où les mangeoires sont présentes. Des photos et une vidéo ont été prises par Germain. Il a remarqué que l'onglet au bout du bec du Cardinal possède une petite excroissance. (CC).

The bird from the south returned again, with more call notes and chasing and once removed, quieter singing followed.

Observation Details: Heard one individual singing, near river, due east from my parking spot. Then heard another individual making loud chip noises simultaneously. Then heard two individuals singing one, then the other, on either side of the river. Eventually saw the near one fly away (an all-red male). Soon heard another individual singing on the far side, a bit farther south. Often  the same call (like a car alarm) was being alternated between the two individuals. ... Heard yet another individual singing from trees just east of levy (due east of my parking spot). ... (Posted audio recording of two individuals singing simultaneously, [including BCCH calls].)

Observation Details: The audio recordings below show the perils of identifying rare birds by sound only. A week ago, I heard what I thought was unmistakaby the song of the Prothonotary Warbler, but I couldn't find it, and since the habitat wasn't right, I decided not to report it. Today, I heard the song again, but this time round, I noticed it always came from the vicinity of a singing Northern Cardinal. After further observations, I came to the conclusion that the song was actually from the Northern Cardinal. In fact, notice from the recordings that the "tweet-tweet-tweet" always comes right after a few notes of the usual Northern Cardinal song. Just for fun, I ran the audio through BirdNet, and it predicted Prothonotary with 100% probability, so I wasn't wrong about the resemblence.

Observation Details: close enough to be able to hear the "warm-up" to dawn song - the cardinal sings a few tentative notes, maybe even goes almost all the way through his song, but then stops abruptly before it's complete. More variation in notes, sometimes sings what sound like song-hybrids between one type of vocalization and another, all abruptly dropped. Generally this little bit of singing is sung from a more secluded place and sung much quieter - then during real dawn song the cardinal flies to an exposed or high-up perch, turns the volume WAY up, and sings stereotyped songs with little to no variation, switching between calls after a few phrases.

Fantastic bird by Chris!  As quick as I could get there, ran into Chris near original location close to dam.   We looked for about 30 minutes along dry creek bed with denser vegetation (willows, Russian Olives, etc..) and along treed fence line paralleling creek on south side.  At about 9:50a Chris heard a chip note just to west of where we were searching.  We both went that way and just as Laurens walked up, I heard a chip along fence line.  Bird then flew up to top of a snag, viewed briefly there.  Female or young male that was not bright red, but rather very pale orangey.  Large red/orange bill seen, crest (although held down), long tail.   Bird almost immediately flew east along fence line into Juniper.  From here, I played chip note, and bird starting singing (recorded).  Recorded singing from here:  35.8933017, -105.9983119.  Unable to get clean photo as it stayed in a position that was mostly obscured entire time after the high snag.  Appears to be a 2nd Co record (per eBird) with other being in Edgewood (Sep 2014).  This record appears to be the furthest recorded north of any records on the west side of the mountains (or I-25).

Second county record! Heard sharp, rather thin, metallic chip which I recognized as familiar but couldn't quite place because of unusual context. Called a few times then hopped into view in lower bare branches of a cottonwood just long enough to see diagnostically before flying downstream into willows. Medium sized passerine with large conical orange-red bill, mostly brownish plumage with some orange and olive tones, with reddish on top of prominent crest and on wings. Tail relatively long. Only similar species is Pyrrhuloxia which has a pale, not reddish, bill and the bill has a strongly curved upper mandible, which this bird did not have. Seen just below the dam west of the parking area, about .8 mile downstream from hotspot/parking. Later, after TJay and Laurens showed up, it sang several times and we all got audio, but poor views while it was hopping around in a juniper well concealed. 35.8937739 , -105.9979546 is the spot where it disappeared into the willows along the dry creek bed, which is a pretty central location for the area within which it wandered on both sides of creek and upstream as far as the dam. Thanks to TJay for the photos and Laurens for the high quality recording. Juvenile male plumage is purported to last only until first fall or winter, so I will assume this was a rather brightly plumaged female.

Media notes: Female calls and male with American Crow calling.  Others are Northern Mockingbird, Carolina Wren.  Note: I record as many birds as I can and I hardly ever use playback.  Not during the nesting season.  That takes energy for the bird and it could be fatal,  and they are trying to care for young at this time.  So please, no playbacks everyone.  I think recording can be one of the least intrusive methods to share our bird encounters and the birds can go on their way.  They have enough to worry about keeping alive out there without our interfering.

Media notes: HIgh-pass filter and amplification applied.
Observation Details: In the field, the (unseen) bird in the foreground of the attached recordings had me completely flummoxed. What I was hearing was loud, alternating pairs of notes on two pitches, low and high: "bo-PEEP, bo-PEEP, bo-PEEP, bo-PEEP." I made several recordings. When listening to them afterward, I noticed that the series of note pairs was sometimes followed by a "chuck-chuck-chuck-chuck-chuck" that sounded like a typical ending to a Northern Cardinal song. Then, after a couple of hours' pause, I played the same recordings again. This time, all I heard was a normal Northen Cardinal song of alternating high and low notes: "WHY-now, WHY-now, WHY-now, WHY-now, chuck-chuck-chuck-chuck-chuck." After pondering the sonograms a bit, I noticed that this particular bird clips the initial high-pitched note very short. Apparently, my perception of its song depends strongly on whether my ear/brain picks up that note or not. It reminds me of the "vase or two faces" or "blue or gold dress" sort of ambiguity, but I don't know whether others will have the same perception.

FOS cardinal song for me. It was just before sunrise and a bit surprising given the cold but the days are getting longer Birds of the World reports song most commonly starts 15 minutes after Civil Twilight  Dec to March in south central Wisconsin, earlier in April. The bird was singing when I pulled into the parking lot at about 0650. Civil twilight started at 0646.
